RECALL: We just learned that asking good questions is a core scientific skill. We know that great scientists ask amazing questions. Now we practise this skill ourselves — by starting from the answer and working our way backwards to the question.

CORE: Look at the answers provided in Activity 1.1. Your task is to come up with a curious, creative, and fun question or situation that could lead to each answer. There are never any wrong questions, so let your imagination run wild! An example to guide you: if the answer is 'Just make it half!' — this could be the answer to 'How do we share cake equally?', or 'My essay is too long!', or 'I cannot fit this in the envelope' — all very different questions leading to the same answer.

VISUAL BRIDGE: Think of it like a treasure hunt in reverse. Normally, the clue leads you to the treasure. In this activity, you are given the treasure — the answer — and you must imagine what clue (question) could have led someone there. Just as there are many paths through a forest to reach the same river, many different questions can lead to the same answer.

WORK-THROUGH: Let us walk through one of the activity's answers together: 'Just add some milk.' Obvious question: 'What is 2 + milk?' — not interesting! Now think creatively: 'My tea is too strong and bitter — what should I do?' Answer: Just add some milk! Or: 'The kheer has become too thick and is sticking to the bottom of the pot — what can I do?' Answer: Just add some milk! Or a biology question: 'What do mammal babies drink for their first nutrition?' Answer: Just add some milk! The same answer emerges from three completely different contexts — a kitchen problem, a cooking accident, and a biology fact. This is exactly the kind of creative, multi-directional thinking that good scientists practise.

ERROR ALERT: Some students worry about giving a 'wrong' question. In this activity, almost no question is wrong — the only weak questions are the obvious ones (like 'What is the answer to an arithmetic problem?') that show no creativity or imagination. The goal is not to find one correct question but to explore as many different possible questions as you can think of. Breadth and creativity are the measures of success here.

media_intent.title: Many Questions, One Answer: The Reverse Thinking Map
media_intent.intent_description: A radial diagram with 'Just add some milk.' in a central box. Five or six branches radiate outward, each showing a different question that leads to the same answer: 'My tea is too strong?', 'The kheer is too thick?', 'What do mammal babies drink?', 'My porridge is dry?', 'My batter is too stiff?'. The visual form of a radial map emphasises that one answer has many possible questions from completely different domains.
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Visually demonstrates the worked example from the text — that a single answer can emerge from very different questions. This primes students for the interactive activity by showing concretely what creative reverse thinking looks like.

media_intent.title: Activity 1.1 Interactive: Question the Answer
media_intent.intent_description: An interactive digital version of Activity 1.1 from the textbook. Students see one answer card at a time (e.g., 'Because the cat's teeth were crooked', 'Just add some milk', 'Don't panic, I have my towel', '42'). They type their creative questions into a text box. After submitting, example responses are revealed — showing 3–4 sample creative questions for each answer from different domains (everyday life, science, humour). Students can compare their questions to the examples.
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Transforms the textbook's paper-based activity into an interactive digital experience. The reveal mechanic (student types first, then sees examples) encourages independent creative thinking before providing scaffolding. Directly serves both apply-level objectives.

media_intent.title: How Scientists Ask Questions in Reverse: Real Examples
media_intent.intent_description: A short animated video (2 min) showing how real scientists often start from an observation (an answer) and work backwards to frame a question. Examples include: Fleming noticing a mould killing bacteria (observation) → 'What is this mould doing?' (question) → penicillin. The video frames reverse thinking as a real scientific habit, not just a classroom game.
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Provides real-world scientific context for the reverse questioning activity. For students who complete the activity quickly or need deeper engagement, this video shows that the skill practised in Activity 1.1 is genuinely used in scientific discovery.

topics.topic_name: Chapter Review — The Ever-Evolving World of Science

learning_objectives.objective_text: Summarise the key ideas introduced in Chapter 1: science as a process, interconnected fields, and the value of questioning
Identify the major topic areas students will explore throughout Grade 7 science

topics.original_chunk: Chapter 1: The Ever-Evolving World of Science — Chapter Review. This chapter introduced science as a process of questioning, observing, and exploring. It previewed the major topics of Grade 7: properties of materials, reversible and irreversible changes, heat and the water cycle, body changes during adolescence, essential life processes, plant nutrition, measurement of time, light and shadows, and movements of the Earth, Moon, and Sun. The chapter concluded with Section 1.1 and Activity 1.1 — Question the Answer — encouraging students to practise the art of asking creative, open-ended scientific questions.
topics.modified_chunk:
HOOK: Let us see how everything we have explored in Chapter 1 connects — from a paper plane in the corridor to eclipses in the sky, from haldi stains in the kitchen to the beating of your heart.

RECALL: We began this chapter with science as a way of thinking and ended with the skill of asking creative questions. Along the way, we previewed everything you will study in Grade 7 Science — materials, changes, life processes, time, light, and the universe.

CORE: Chapter 1, 'The Ever-Evolving World of Science', is an overview and an invitation. It introduces science as a process of questioning, observing, and exploring — not a fixed collection of facts. It previews all the major topic areas: properties and classification of materials, reversible and irreversible changes, heat and the water cycle, adolescence and life processes, plant nutrition, time and speed, light and shadows, Earth-Moon-Sun movements, and the skill of scientific questioning.

VISUAL BRIDGE: Think of Chapter 1 as the index map you see at the beginning of a long trek. It shows you all the trails, the rivers, the peaks, and the forests you will explore over the year. Each trail is a chapter in the textbook. Right now, you are standing at the starting point, map in hand — and the entire Grade 7 Science journey is spread out before you. Every topic you just previewed is a destination waiting to be explored in depth.

WORK-THROUGH: Let us connect the key ideas across the chapter to see how they fit together. Science is a way of thinking (T1) that requires curiosity and questioning (T2) and a sense of responsibility towards the environment (T3). It cuts across interconnected branches — physics, chemistry, biology, earth sciences (T4). These branches bring us questions about materials (T5, T6), changes (T7, T8), life in living things (T9, T10, T11), the measurement of time, light, and cosmic movements (T12, T13, T14). Experiments generate more questions, not just answers (T15). And the best scientists are those who ask the most imaginative questions (T16, T17). Together, these ideas define what it means to be a young scientist at the start of Grade 7.

ERROR ALERT: Students sometimes think of a review chapter like this as a 'light' or 'easy' chapter that can be skipped or speed-read. In fact, Chapter 1 builds the entire scientific mindset that all subsequent chapters depend on. The ideas here — curiosity, questioning, interconnection, responsibility, and the iterative nature of science — are not just introductory. They are the foundation on which everything else in Grade 7 Science is built.

media_intent.title: Chapter 1 Map: The Grade 7 Science Journey Ahead
media_intent.intent_description: A visual 'trek map' of the Grade 7 Science journey. A central starting point is labelled 'Chapter 1: The Ever-Evolving World of Science'. From it, eight paths radiate outward, each labelled with a major topic area: Materials & Properties, Metals & Non-Metals, Reversible/Irreversible Changes, Heat & Water Cycle, Adolescence & Life Processes, Plant Nutrition, Time & Motion, Light & the Universe. Each path ends in a small icon representing that topic. The whole image feels like a map of adventures ahead.
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Provides a memorable overview visual that students can return to throughout the year. Directly serves the remember-level objective of identifying major topic areas. The map metaphor reinforces the chapter's framing of science as an exploration.

media_intent.title: The Three Big Ideas of Chapter 1
media_intent.intent_description: A clean three-pillar infographic. Each pillar shows one big idea from Chapter 1: Pillar 1 — 'Science is a Way of Thinking' (icon: a thinking child with a question mark), Pillar 2 — 'All Branches of Science are Connected' (icon: the banyan tree from T4), Pillar 3 — 'Questions are as Important as Answers' (icon: an open question mark transforming into a lightbulb). Below all three pillars: 'These three ideas are the foundation of everything you will learn in Grade 7 Science.'
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Consolidates the three core conceptual ideas from the chapter into a single visual summary. Supports the summarise-level objective and provides a review scaffold that students can use for self-checking before or after formal assessment.

media_intent.title: Chapter 1 Concept Map: Connect the Topics
media_intent.intent_description: An interactive concept-mapping activity where students are given 12–14 labelled nodes (one per major topic from the chapter) and asked to drag connecting arrows between nodes that are related. For example, 'Science as a Way of Thinking' → 'Curiosity and Questioning' → 'Activity 1.1'. Students can label their arrows with short phrases. After completing, a suggested completed map is revealed for comparison.
media_intent.pedagogical_purpose: Actively engages students in making connections between the 18 topics of the chapter — serving the summarise-level objective through a construct activity rather than passive recall. The comparison to a model map provides self-assessment.
